title: Four-day week for a 24/7 support team

critical_claims.proposition: The pilot supports that a 32-hour schedule can preserve response time and customer satisfaction for selected agents outside peak season.
The 9% tickets-per-hour increase proves total sustainable productivity will rise for all 65 agents.
The organization can maintain 24/7 coverage and service levels through peak season without additional hiring.
The schedule will improve retention across the full team.
The pilot establishes that workload intensity and support quality remain acceptable.
The company should make the schedule permanent for all agents before a representative peak-season trial, quality audit, fairness review, and employment-rule review.

common_false_claims.claim: The pilot proves a four-day week caused a 28% reduction in sick days.
common_false_claims.reason: The small volunteer pilot was not randomized and does not establish causality.

common_false_claims.claim: Customer service was unchanged on every measure.
common_false_claims.reason: Response time, satisfaction, and backlog moved differently, and quality-error rates were not measured.

common_false_claims.claim: No extra hiring will be needed during peak season.
common_false_claims.reason: Peak season was excluded from the pilot.

source_pack.title: Authored twelve-week support schedule pilot summary

acceptable_unresolved_outcomes.reason: Volunteer selection and missing quality data limit generalization.
The pilot is too small and short to establish retention effects.
Quality-error and workload-intensity measures were not collected.
